title: Plane-Based Writing and Script Typology
subtitle: Where SignWriting fits, and where existing writing-system categories strain
summary: SignWriting is best understood through a two-axis typology: what writing represents and how the written unit is organized for reading.
synopsis: SignWriting is best understood through a two-axis typological account: one axis asks what linguistic or embodied structure writing represents, and the other asks how the written unit is organized for reading. Plane-based writing names the organization of written units, distinguishes SignWriting from pictorial drawing, featural script, notation, and encoding, and shows where inherited writing-system categories strain without needing to be discarded.
cover_image_alt: A scholarly desk with abstract sequential marks opening into a spatial signed-writing plane, typology cards, an open manuscript, and geometric writing fields.

slides.title: The Typology Problem
slides.summary: SignWriting strains familiar writing-system categories because its written unit is organized spatially rather than only sequentially.

image_prompts.concrete: Scholarly writing-systems slide comparing alphabetic line writing, character blocks, notation, and a spatial SignWriting signbox
image_prompts.abstract: A line of text opening into a two-dimensional written field

bullets: The question needs a second axis
slides.speech: The paper begins with a typology problem. Existing writing-system categories remain useful, but they often assume that the written unit is primarily organized through sequence. SignWriting exposes a missing question: how is the written unit organized for reading?
slides.notes: Do not dismiss existing typology. The argument is extension and pressure, not replacement.

slides.title: The Missing Axis
slides.summary: A two-axis account separates what writing represents from how the written unit is organized.

image_prompts.concrete: Two-axis typology slide with representational axis and organizational axis, placing SignWriting in a plane-based region
image_prompts.abstract: Two crossing axes locating a composed written sign

bullets: Organizational axis: how the written unit is arranged
It does not replace representational typology
slides.speech: The key move is to separate two questions. One asks what the writing represents. The other asks how the written unit is organized. Plane-based writing names the organizational side of SignWriting without pretending that organization is the whole theory.
slides.notes: This is the central conceptual slide.

slides.title: What SignWriting Is Not
slides.summary: The paper distinguishes SignWriting from pictorial drawing, featural script, notation, and encoding.

image_prompts.concrete: Comparison slide with four rejected reductions: pictorial drawing, featural script, notation, and encoding, contrasted with plane-based writing
image_prompts.abstract: Four simplified labels set aside around a stable writing-system center

slides.speech: The paper also clears away reductions. SignWriting is not merely pictorial drawing, not merely a featural script, not merely notation, and not merely an encoding. Each comparison reveals something, but none of them is sufficient alone.
slides.notes: Keep this comparative rather than defensive.

slides.title: Typological Contribution
slides.summary: SignWriting contributes a clearer case for plane-based writing and prepares the series for authored space, legibility, handwriting, and encoding.

image_prompts.concrete: Reading path slide moving from plane-based writing to authored space, legibility, handwriting, machine encoding, and kinematography
image_prompts.abstract: A plane-based written sign opening into the next theoretical papers

bullets: Plane-based writing is an analytic descriptor
The next question is authored space
slides.speech: The contribution is bounded but important. Plane-based writing gives a clearer descriptor for one part of SignWriting's structure. It pressures inherited categories without discarding them. The next paper then asks what the writer actually authors inside that plane.
slides.notes: Close by pointing to Perspective and Authored Space.

slides.title: Claims And Limits
slides.summary: Plane-based writing names an organizational axis; it does not replace all writing-system typology or settle every empirical question.

image_prompts.concrete: Two-axis writing typology slide with a clear boundary around plane-based organization and separate open boxes for evidence, reading research, and encoding
image_prompts.abstract: A plane-based written sign located on one axis while other questions remain open

bullets: Names organization, not the whole theory
Does not prove literacy outcomes by itself
slides.speech: The bounded claim is important. Plane-based writing names an organizational axis. It does not replace all existing typology, prove literacy outcomes by itself, or settle every technical question. Its value is that it makes the next questions clearer: authored space, legibility, handwriting, and encoding.
slides.notes: This slide protects the central term from becoming too large. Keep the tone confident and disciplined.

companion_documents.label: Point, Line, and Plane Evidence Notes
